quote: Well, I've you know, I have been checking it around, you know, you know, and none of that. My electric bill keeps going up.
stewart_read: Darla volunteers her motivation unprompted — she's been shopping around and her bill is the driver. The schema marks interest_in_solar as the highest-weighted verify item with explicit depth_grading: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Holland's response is two sentences of sympathy and an immediate pivot to utility company. The emotional anchor — the reason Darla is on this call at all — is acknowledged and abandoned. This is not a strategic deferral; there is no later moment in the transcript where Holland returns to Darla's motivation.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been conserving time given the service-area uncertainty she was about to surface. But the supervisor check doesn't happen until 03:40 — there was time to go deeper here.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask Holland: 'What did Darla tell you about why she wanted solar?' If she can't reconstruct it beyond 'her bill is high,' that's the gap. The schema's depth_grading on this item means it's not a checkbox — it's the emotional foundation the rest of the call builds on. A suggested direction (not a verbatim script): stay in Darla's frustration for 30 more seconds. 'You've been checking around — what's been holding you back from pulling the trigger?' That question surfaces competitive context AND deepens the emotional anchor simultaneously.

stewart_read: Fifteen years is the schema's hard_dq_if threshold for roof age. Holland captures the number and moves on without probing whether Darla has replacement plans. Two things are missed simultaneously: (1) the DQ check — is this roof actually over 15 years, and if so, is replacement planned? Darla said 'about fifteen,' which could mean 14 or 16; Holland never clarifies. (2) The roofing cross-sell signal — if the roof needs replacement, Ion does roofing in-house, and this is a bridge opportunity. The schema's cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned section explicitly flags this scenario.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged 'about fifteen' as within threshold and moved on deliberately. But the schema's hard_dq_if condition requires confirmation, not assumption — and the roofing bridge is available regardless of whether the roof is a DQ.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the schema's roof_type_age coaching note and the cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned template. The question she needed to ask: 'Do you have any plans to replace that roof in the next few years?' If yes, that's a roofing conversation. If no, the DQ risk is real and needs to be flagged for the closer. Either way, Holland needed one more question here.

quote: Sometimes it's 700. Sometimes it's 500. Let's see. I have all electric.
stewart_read: Darla volunteers a $500–$700 range and the all-electric detail in the same breath — this is the highest-value data point in the call. Holland's response is 'Oh my goodness' followed by 'Yeah. Okay. Alrighty.' The schema's bill_anchoring section is explicit: capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. Holland captures and drops. The flip was available: Darla's own words ('my electric bill keeps going up,' said at 02:41) plus the $500–$700 range plus all-electric is a complete setup for the inversion move. Holland never takes it.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been holding the flip for after the service-area check resolved, to avoid building energy on a lead that might not qualify. But the supervisor check doesn't happen until 03:40, and there is no later moment in the transcript where Holland returns to the bill pain.
coaching_implication: This is the universal floor coaching gap the schema identifies: 'Across all 7 deep-read calls, every rep captured the bill anchor and NONE flipped it.' Use this moment to introduce the objection_inversion framework. The flip here doesn't require a number — it requires redirecting Darla's frustration at the utility rather than at solar's cost. Suggested direction (intent, not verbatim): 'Five to seven hundred dollars a month — that's the bill solar is designed to replace. The question isn't whether solar costs money; it's whether what you're paying BGE right now is worth keeping.' The emotion is the leverage, not the arithmetic.

quote: Sorry. Is there I'm just asking. So it looks like you're just a tiny, tiny, tiny bit out of our bounds. So I'm just reaching out to see if it would be okay if we work with you.
stewart_read: Holland discloses a service-area boundary issue to Darla without any prepared framing. 'Tiny, tiny, tiny bit out of our bounds' is improvised and anxiety-signaling — the triple repetition communicates uncertainty, not confidence. Darla has no context for what 'out of our bounds' means, why it was checked, or what the resolution means for her. The issue resolves ('everything looks good') but Holland never explains what was checked or why Darla should feel confident proceeding. This is a trust moment that was handled procedurally but not relationally.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been deliberately minimizing the issue to avoid alarming Darla. The 'tiny, tiny, tiny' framing could be a conscious softening move. But the absence of any explanation after the resolution ('everything looks good here on my end') suggests this was anxiety management, not strategy.
coaching_implication: The service-area check itself is correct procedure. The coaching gap is in the framing before and after. Before: if Holland needs to put a customer on hold to check eligibility, she should have a prepared line — something like 'Let me just confirm we service your area — one moment.' After: when the check clears, close the loop for the customer. 'Good news — you're in our service area, so we're all set to move forward.' Darla never got that close.

quote: No. It should be around that.
stewart_read: Darla's response to the 670 threshold is 'around that' — a soft non-confirmation Holland accepts without probing. The schema's spencer_operator_note is directly relevant: stated credit is unreliable (customers see Vantage scores, not FICO; three bureaus differ by 30–50 points). 'Around that' could mean 650 or 690 — Holland doesn't know, and neither does Darla. Holland accepts the ambiguity and moves on. The schema's open policy question (should the setter DQ on stated credit, or pass to the closer's pull?) means the severity of this gap depends on Ion's current policy — but the failure to probe is a coaching gap regardless.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged 'around that' as sufficient confirmation given the ambiguity of stated credit. But 'around that' is not a confirmation — it's a hedge. A single follow-up question ('Do you know roughly what range you're in?') would have surfaced more information without DQ'ing the lead.
coaching_implication: Flag for the manager to confirm Ion's current credit-DQ policy (schema section qualifiers.credit_qualifier, spencer_operator_note). If the policy is 'let the closer's pull decide,' Holland should be coached to pass 'around that' responses through without treating them as confirmations. If the policy is 'setter DQs on stated,' Holland needed one more probe here. Either way, accepting 'around that' without a follow-up is a gap.

stewart_read: Darla self-corrects to 40 years at 01:29. Forty-year tenure in a single-family home is a strong qualifier signal — long-tenure homeowners are high-value solar prospects (stable ownership, no near-term move risk, deep familiarity with the property's energy patterns). Holland notes it ('Wow. Okay. That's a really long time') and moves immediately to the next verify item. The signal is acknowledged but not anchored — there is no bridge to what 40 years of BGE bills represents, no tie to the bill pain Darla will disclose at 03:06.
coaching_implication: Show Holland how tenure and bill pain connect. Forty years of rising BGE bills is not just a verify data point — it's the emotional context for everything that follows. A suggested direction (intent, not verbatim): 'Forty years — so you've watched that bill climb the whole time. What's it been like lately?' That question bridges tenure to bill pain and sets up the anchor Holland never builds.

quote: Yeah. So what we could do is, I can just send you a text message. And then whenever you get home today, if you could just send me a photo of that, then we'd be able to finish up this design for you.
stewart_read: Holland's pivot to a text follow-up is the correct procedural move when the bill is unavailable. The gap is in the execution: no time anchor ('whenever you get home today' is not a commitment), no locked callback, no appointment set, no 'is that fair' handshake, and no button-up elements from the schema. The call ends with 'we will keep in touch' — the softest possible close. Whether this is a critical gap depends on lead type: if paid, the bill is required before the appointment can be set, and the open loop is the correct interim step — but it still needed a time anchor. If organic, Holland could have booked the appointment now and deferred bill collection to the specialist, making the open loop an unnecessary delay. The manager should confirm lead type.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that booking an appointment without the bill would create a fragile sit. On a paid lead, that judgment is correct procedure. But even on a paid lead, 'whenever you get home today' is not a time anchor — it's an open invitation to forget.
coaching_implication: Regardless of lead type, the close needed a time anchor. Suggested direction (intent, not verbatim): 'When do you expect to be home today? I'll follow up at [specific time] to make sure we get that photo and get you on the calendar.' That converts an open loop into a soft appointment. If organic, coach Holland to book the sit now and note 'bill to be collected by specialist on site.'

quote: Alright. Well, yeah, I have you down, Darla, as the property owner. Is there anybody else that's entitled to the home with you?
stewart_read: Holland executes the title_co_owners verify item correctly and Darla confirms she is the sole owner. No spouse protocol is triggered because none applies. This is a clean verify execution — Holland asked the right question, got a clear answer, and moved on. Flagged here only to confirm the protocol was checked, not missed.
coaching_implication: No coaching gap here — this is correct execution of verify.title_co_owners. The manager can use this as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1: Holland did ask the title question cleanly. The coaching conversation is about what happened with the other verify items, not this one.
